text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY - INTERIM TREATMENT ASSESSMENT NOTE
Neoadjuvant Therapy Cycle 2 Follow-Up Visit

PATIENT: Marisol Fuentes-Delgado
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology / Breast Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic woman with newly diagnosed stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), BRCA1-mutated, presenting for scheduled interim assessment following completion of cycle 2 of dose-dense Adriamycin/Cytoxan (ddAC) in combination with pembro (KEYNOTE-522 regimen, neoadjuvant phase). She is here for toxicity assessment, clinical response evaluation, and to discuss ongoing management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado was diagnosed with left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative (ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 1+ by IHC, not amplified by FISH), Ki-67 92%, at a primary tumor size of 3.4 cm with one biopsy-confirmed left axillary lymph node metastasis (T2N1M0, stage IIB, AJCC 8th edition). Tumor demonstrated basal-like immunophenotype (CK5/6+, EGFR 2+) and high-grade histology (Nottingham 9/9) with lymphovascular invasion. PD-L1 CPS was 28 by 22C3 assay. Germline genetic testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74). Somatic NGS (FoundationOne CDx, 500-gene panel) on the primary tumor confirmed TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%), BRCA1 biallelic inactivation (germline + somatic LOH), PTEN homozygous deletion, MYC high-level amplification (copy number 18.4), RB1 frameshift loss, and CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion. HRD score was 62 (above the threshold of 42), consistent with the genomic instability expected from BRCA1 loss. TMB was 9 mut/Mb (intermediate) and MSS. Staging workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, bone scan) showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. ECOG performance status was 1 at diagnosis.

Given the diagnosis of stage IIB TNBC with PD-L1 CPS 28, the patient was initiated on neoadjuvant Keytruda 200 mg IV every 3 weeks concurrent with ddAC (doxo 60 mg/m2 + CTX 600 mg/m2 every 2 weeks, planned for 4 cycles), followed by planned transition to weekly Taxol 80 mg/m2 for 12 weeks with continued pembro, per KEYNOTE-522 protocol. Filgrastim was prescribed for myeloid support given the dose-dense schedule. Baseline echocardiogram showed LVEF 62%.

Cycle 1 of ddAC/pembro was administered without hypersensitivity reaction. After cycle 1, she experienced grade 3 ANC nadir (0.8 x10^9/L), managed with filgrastim dose adjustment without need for dose reduction. She also developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 2 nausea/vomiting, as well as grade 1 alopecia (progressing to total alopecia by cycle 2). She was noted to have grade 1 peripheral neuropathy of the fingertips at that time, attributed to early neuropathic sensitization in the setting of poorly controlled hyperglycemia. Endocrinology was consulted, HbA1c was found to be 8.4%, and metformin was uptitrated. No immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from pembro were identified at cycle 1 assessment.

Cycle 2 of ddAC/Keytruda was administered on schedule. She presents today approximately 3 weeks after cycle 2 infusion (week 6 of treatment). Overall, she is tolerating therapy with manageable, though bothersome, side effects.

Since last visit, she reports:
- Ongoing fatigue, which she describes as moderate and persistent; she is resting more and has reduced household activities but is ambulatory and functional
- Nausea has improved compared to after cycle 1; she is eating small, frequent meals with reasonable oral intake
- Alopecia now complete; she reports significant emotional distress related to hair loss, which was "very hard" for her psychologically; she is using a wig
- No fevers, chills, or signs of infection
- No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea at rest
- Blood pressure has been elevated at home readings (reported values of 150-160/90s mmHg); she has been compliant with lisinopril
- Fingertip tingling continues, unchanged from prior assessment; she denies any significant functional impairment from this
- She notes the breast mass "feels smaller" to her own palpation, consistent with the clinical exam below
- No new symptoms referable to lymph nodes, bones, or abdomen

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Significant fatigue (grade 2), decreased appetite (improved), no fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss beyond expected treatment-related changes, no night sweats.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mild dry mouth; no mucositis at this time; complete alopecia as noted.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no orthopnea, no lower extremity edema. Blood pressure elevated per above.
Respiratory: No cough, no dyspnea at rest, mild exertional dyspnea on stair climbing (baseline per patient).
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea, improved from cycle 1; no vomiting; no diarrhea; no constipation (on stool softener); no abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria. Patient is postmenopausal.
Musculoskeletal: No new joint pain or bone pain beyond pre-existing bilateral knee osteoarthritis.
Neurological: Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy with fingertip tingling bilaterally; no numbness in feet; no gait disturbance.
Dermatological: Complete alopecia. Mild skin dryness. No rash.
Endocrine: Polyuria/polydipsia reduced compared to presentation (improved glycemic control with uptitrated metformin).
Psychiatric: Significant anxiety and emotional distress related to diagnosis and treatment; patient tearful during visit; supportive counseling provided.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Triple-negative breast cancer, stage IIB (T2N1M0), left breast, BRCA1-mutated - currently on neoadjuvant therapy as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - on metformin (dose recently increased); HbA1c 8.4% at last check, prior 7.8% at diagnosis workup; endocrinology co-managing
3. Hypertension - on lisinopril; blood pressure suboptimally controlled today
4. Obesity - BMI 33.2 at diagnosis
5. Postmenopausal status
6. Bilateral knee osteoarthritis - mild, managed with acetaminophen PRN

No prior breast surgeries or biopsies. No history of prior malignancy. No prior chemotherapy or radiation therapy.

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: Breast cancer diagnosed at age 42 (premenopausal); deceased
- Maternal aunt: Ovarian cancer
- Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed in patient (c.5266dupC); genetic counseling completed prior to treatment initiation; patient's adult children offered referral for cascade testing

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic woman. She is married and lives at home with her husband. She has two adult children who are involved and supportive. She is a retired administrative assistant. Former smoker with approximately 10 pack-year history; quit approximately 15 years ago. Denies current alcohol use. Denies illicit drug use. No occupational exposures of note. Born in Mexico, has lived in the United States for over 30 years; English-language proficiency adequate for medical discussions, Spanish interpretation offered and available.

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin: rash (mild, remote - tolerated amoxicillin without incident per patient report, allergy label retained for documentation purposes)
- No known drug allergies to current medications or chemotherapy agents

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily with meals (held on chemotherapy administration days per institutional protocol)
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (dose recently increased from 5 mg due to suboptimal blood pressure control)
3. Filgrastim 480 mcg subcutaneous injection daily for 7 days following each ddAC cycle (prophylactic, dose-dense schedule support)
4. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours PRN nausea (first 3 days post-chemotherapy)
5. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally every 6 hours PRN nausea (breakthrough)
6. Dexamethasone 8 mg orally twice daily for 3 days post-chemotherapy (antiemetic, tapering per protocol)
7. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally twice daily (constipation prevention, dexamethasone/ondansetron effect)
8. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours PRN pain/headache
9. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (gastroprotection)
10. Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) 100 mg orally daily (for neuropathy prophylaxis)
11. Pegfilgrastim was discussed but patient remains on short-acting filgrastim per current protocol
Note: Keytruda 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (most recent dose administered with cycle 2 ddAC)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 148/92 mmHg (elevated; rechecked at end of visit 145/90 mmHg), Heart rate 82 bpm (regular), Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, Temperature 36.8°C (oral), SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 91.4 kg (baseline 92.1 kg at treatment start, -0.7 kg), BMI 33.1.

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued woman. Wearing a wig. Cooperative and engaged in discussion, though tearful at points during conversation about her prognosis and treatment. No acute distress.

Head/Neck: Normocephalic. Total alopecia. No scalp lesions. Sclerae anicteric, conjunctivae pink with mild pallor. Oropharynx moist, no mucositis, no thrush. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated. Thyroid not enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Bilateral radial pulses 2+ and equal.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles.

Breast Exam (with patient's consent, performed in gown):
- Left breast: A palpable firm mass in the 12 o'clock position, upper outer quadrant, measuring approximately 2.0 cm in greatest dimension on palpation (compared to 3.4 cm on initial diagnostic ultrasound and 3.4 cm on diagnostic mammogram). This represents a clinically meaningful reduction in tumor size, consistent with response to therapy. No skin dimpling, peau d'orange, or nipple retraction. No erythema. No ulceration. Prior biopsy site healing appropriately.
- Right breast: No palpable mass or asymmetry.
- Left axilla: No palpable adenopathy. Previously reported suspicious left axillary lymph node is NOT palpable on today's examination. This is a notable clinical improvement from the pre-treatment exam where a firm, palpable 1.8 cm lymph node was appreciated.
- Right axilla: No adenopathy.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No erythema of the feet or hands. Bilateral knees with mild crepitus, non-tender to palpation.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation to light touch intact in lower extremities. Reduced sensation to light touch at bilateral fingertips (grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, consistent with prior documentation). Gait normal.

Skin: Total alopecia. Mild generalized skin dryness. No rash, no erythema, no urticaria. No palmar or plantar changes. No jaundice.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (drawn today, or from last 7 days):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal; ANC 3.9 x10^9/L - recovering well post-cycle 2, filgrastim support)
- Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (grade 1 anemia; slightly below prior nadir)
- Hematocrit: 32.1%
- Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)
- MCV: 87 fL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Chloride: 101 mEq/L
- CO2: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (baseline 1.2; CrCl estimated 52 mL/min by CKD-EPI - stable, consistent with prior)
- Glucose: 142 mg/dL (fasting per patient report; elevated, consistent with poorly controlled T2DM, improved from prior)
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL
- ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
- AST: 32 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (normal)

Endocrine / Other:
- HbA1c: 8.4% (consistent with recently identified suboptimal glycemic control; metformin dose was already increased; endocrinology follow-up scheduled)
- TSH: 2.1 mU/L (normal; no evidence of immune-related thyroid dysfunction at this time)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Note: LFTs and renal function within acceptable parameters for continuation of protocol therapy. No evidence of hepatotoxicity or nephrotoxicity. No elevation of aminotransferases concerning for immune hepatitis.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Clinical breast exam demonstrates significant reduction in palpable tumor as detailed above. The next planned imaging assessment is a breast MRI after completion of cycle 4 of ddAC (mid-neoadjuvant MRI), scheduled in approximately 4-5 weeks per protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old woman with stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, grade 3, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant, PD-L1 CPS 28, HRD-high (score 62), currently receiving neoadjuvant pembro 200 mg IV q3 weeks plus dose-dense AC per KEYNOTE-522 protocol. She is at week 6, post-cycle 2 of ddAC/pembro.

1. DISEASE STATUS / CLINICAL RESPONSE:
Clinically encouraging early response. The left breast mass is significantly smaller on today's exam - estimated at approximately 2.0 cm vs. 3.4 cm at diagnosis, representing an approximate 40% decrease in palpable tumor dimension. Notably, the previously palpable and FNA-confirmed left axillary lymph node is no longer palpable today. While we cannot establish pathologic response based on clinical exam alone, this is a very promising early clinical sign. She is responding in both the primary and nodal compartments. I am cautiously optimistic. We will continue on protocol. Formal radiologic reassessment with breast MRI is planned after completion of cycle 4 of ddAC, as per the KEYNOTE-522 schema. If she achieves a complete clinical and radiologic response by that point, we will continue to planned surgery, but the pathology at the time of resection will ultimately determine the need for adjuvant intensification.
   - Plan: Continue current regimen per protocol; mid-neoadjuvant MRI after cycle 4 ddAC.

2. TREATMENT - CYCLE 3 ddAC/pembro:
The patient is cleared to proceed with cycle 3 of dose-dense Adriamycin 60 mg/m2 IV plus Cytoxan 600 mg/m2 IV today (no dose reduction required based on current counts and organ function). pembro 200 mg IV will be administered concurrently. Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, fosaprepitant per standard protocol. Filgrastim 480 mcg SC to be initiated 24-48 hours post-chemotherapy and continued for 7 days. Metformin held on chemotherapy administration days per protocol.
   - Cumulative anthracycline dose: 120 mg/m2 after this cycle (total planned 240 mg/m2 over 4 cycles of ddAC; within acceptable limits)
   - Baseline echocardiogram (LVEF 62%) documented; cardiac monitoring to be repeated mid-treatment per institutional protocol
   - Plan: Administer cycle 3 as above.

3. HEMATOLOGIC TOXICITY (Prior Grade 3 Neutropenia):
ANC recovered appropriately to 3.9 x10^9/L today with filgrastim support. No dose reduction required at this time. Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.8 x10^9/L) after cycle 1 was expected given dose-dense schedule, particularly in the setting of borderline renal function; filgrastim protocol was optimized. No febrile neutropenia has occurred to date, which is reassuring. Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL reflects mild anemia - will monitor; no transfusion indicated at this time.
   - Plan: Continue filgrastim per protocol. Monitor CBC at each cycle. Threshold for considering pegfilgrastim substitution or further dose adjustment if febrile neutropenia occurs.

4. NAUSEA/VOMITING (Grade 2, Improving):
Nausea significantly improved after cycle 2 compared to cycle 1, suggesting antiemetic regimen optimization with fosaprepitant, ondansetron, and dexamethasone is effective. Patient using prochlorperazine breakthrough PRN with good results.
   - Plan: Continue current antiemetic protocol. Reinforce taking scheduled antiemetics for the first 3-5 days post-infusion (acute and delayed nausea). Small, frequent meals encouraged. Maintain hydration.

5. FATIGUE (Grade 2):
Multifactorial fatigue in the setting of chemotherapy, mild anemia, suboptimally controlled hyperglycemia, and emotional distress. Grade 2 fatigue is common during ddAC therapy and at this point does not warrant dose modification.
   - Plan: Encourage light physical activity as tolerated (walking 10-20 minutes daily if possible). Optimize glycemic control (see below). Iron studies to rule out iron deficiency contributing to anemia ordered. Address psychological component (see item 9).

6. HYPERTENSION:
Blood pressure elevated today at 148/92 mmHg, with patient reporting home readings consistently in the 150s/90s. This represents suboptimal blood pressure control in the context of existing cardiovascular risk and concurrent Adriamycin therapy (cardioprotection important). Lisinopril was recently increased; if not adequately controlling BP in next 2-3 weeks, will consider adding amlodipine.
   - Plan: Increase lisinopril dose from 10 mg to 20 mg daily. Recheck BP at cycle 3 visit (in 2 weeks). Patient counseled on low-sodium diet. Home BP monitoring log to be reviewed. Avoid NSAIDs (can raise BP and worsen renal function). Cardiology referral considered if BP remains uncontrolled.

7. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
HbA1c 8.4%, elevated. Improved glycemic control is important to reduce neuropathy risk and optimize overall treatment tolerance. Endocrinology has been consulted and is co-managing. Metformin was recently uptitrated.
   - Plan: Continue metformin at increased dose. Defer to endocrinology for additional medication adjustments. Hold metformin on chemotherapy days as per protocol. Fasting glucose checks at home encouraged. Low glycemic index diet counseling reinforced.

8. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (Grade 1):
Bilateral fingertip tingling, unchanged from prior assessment. This likely reflects early chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy amplified by suboptimal glycemic control. Grade 1 is manageable and does not require dose modification at this time. Of note, Taxol will be introduced in the taxane phase (post-ddAC); we must monitor neuropathy carefully as this will likely worsen when taxane is added, particularly in a patient with pre-existing hyperglycemia-associated neuropathic predisposition.
   - Plan: Continue pyridoxine supplementation. Optimize glucose control. Monitor neuropathy closely at each visit. If grade 2 or higher neuropathy develops prior to taxane phase, will address proactively. Duloxetine to be considered if neuropathic pain develops.

9. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENTS (irAE) SURVEILLANCE:
No evidence of irAEs at this time. TSH and free T4 are normal (no thyroiditis). LFTs normal (no immune hepatitis). No rash, no diarrhea, no respiratory symptoms. PD-L1 CPS 28, which reflects a tumor with significant immune infiltration and should predict good response to pembro, though also potentially higher irAE risk.
   - Plan: Continue comprehensive irAE surveillance at each visit. Counsel patient regarding symptoms of irAEs - particularly thyroid dysfunction (fatigue, weight changes, palpitations), colitis (diarrhea, abdominal cramping), pneumonitis (cough, dyspnea), and rash. Repeat thyroid function at next cycle.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL DISTRESS:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is visibly distressed regarding her diagnosis, her hair loss, and uncertainty about her future. She expressed significant anxiety and fear today. This is a newly diagnosed patient with a high-risk breast cancer in the neoadjuvant setting, and emotional distress at this stage is expected and validated. She has strong family support.
    - Plan: Referral placed to oncology social work for psychosocial support and resource navigation. Discussed patient support groups for breast cancer / TNBC. Provided educational materials on BRCA1 and hereditary cancer in the family (reiterated referral for cascade testing for adult children). The body image impact of mastectomy (she is informed this is likely forthcoming) and alopecia were acknowledged. Psychiatric or psychology referral to be placed if mood symptoms worsen or meet criteria for major depressive disorder at next assessment.

11. GERMLINE BRCA1 STATUS AND FUTURE CONSIDERATIONS:
Patient has confirmed pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC). Genetic counseling was completed prior to treatment. Surgical planning will include formal discussion with breast surgery regarding bilateral mastectomy options (including contralateral prophylactic mastectomy given germline status). Depending on pathologic response, adjuvant ola (per OlympiA trial data) and adjuvant Xeloda (per CREATE-X, for residual disease) will be considerations at the time of post-operative planning.
    - Plan: Breast surgery referral confirmed and consultation appointment scheduled for approximately week 18 (after completion of all neoadjuvant therapy). Will revisit PARP inhibitor and adjuvant cape discussions at that time based on pathologic response.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
Cycle 3 of ddAC/Keytruda today. Patient to return in 2 weeks for cycle 4 pre-treatment assessment. Mid-neoadjuvant breast MRI to be scheduled approximately 2-3 weeks after cycle 4 administration. Urgency precautions reviewed: patient instructed to call or present to the emergency department for fever > 38.3°C, intractable nausea/vomiting, severe diarrhea, chest pain, dyspnea, or any new neurologic symptoms.

Time spent with patient: Approximately 55 minutes (including counseling, examination, and coordination of care).

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Department of Hematology/Oncology
[Institution Name]
